title: Ebonizing wood with vinegar and steel wool
subtitle: Chemically darken high-tannin timber using iron acetate solution
excerpt: How to ebonize oak, walnut, or chestnut using a solution of steel wool dissolved in white vinegar: preparing the solution, preparing the timber surface, applying the solution and watching the colour develop, and sealing the finished surface.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. The Mechanic's Workshop Companion. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of chemically darkening wood to resemble ebony. Using iron acetate solution from dissolved steel wool in vinegar, the iron reacts with tannins in the wood to produce a dark grey to black colour. No paint or stain; the colour is in the wood itself.

A naturally occurring polyphenol compound present in high concentrations in oak, walnut, chestnut, and cherry. It reacts with iron acetate solution to produce the dark colour in ebonizing. Low-tannin woods such as pine and beech produce little or no colour change.

content.text: is a chemical process, not a surface coating. The iron acetate in the solution reacts with

in the wood fibres to produce a dark colour that sits within the grain rather than on top of it. The final tone ranges from grey-black on oak to near-black on walnut depending on the wood's natural tannin content, the concentration of the solution, and the number of coats.

Pull apart a pad of 0000 or 000 grade steel wool and place it in a glass jar with a loosely fitted lid (not a sealed lid; the reaction produces gas). Pour over enough white wine vinegar or cider vinegar to submerge the wool completely. Leave for 1 to 3 days until the wool has mostly dissolved and the liquid turns amber to dark brown. Strain the solution through a coffee filter into a second jar before use; any particles left in the solution will rust on the wood surface.

Sand the timber to 150 grit using the sanding block, always working with the grain. Wipe down with a cloth dampened with methylated spirit to remove sanding dust and any residual oil or wax. Allow the surface to dry for 30 minutes. Any old finish or grease on the surface will block the solution from reaching the wood; full removal is needed for an even result.

Apply the iron acetate solution with a soft brush or sponge, working quickly with the grain. The colour will begin to develop within 2 to 5 minutes on high-tannin wood. Allow the first coat to dry fully (20 to 30 minutes), then sand very lightly with 220 grit on the sanding block to knock down any raised grain fibres. Apply a second coat. Repeat until the depth of colour meets the target. Low-tannin wood can be treated first with a coat of strong black tea (which adds tannin to the surface) and allowed to dry before the iron acetate is applied.

Once the final coat is dry, the surface needs sealing to prevent continued oxidation and to give a finished appearance. A wax finish gives a flat, natural look; shellac gives a warmer sheen; water-based lacquer gives maximum protection. Whichever sealer is chosen, apply it in thin coats and allow full drying between coats. The ebonized surface is stable once sealed.
